fr.elements: Main Building; Front Driveway; Landscaped Area; Perimeter Fencing
fr.considerations: Single Access Point; Limited Rear Access
fr.recommendations: Enhancing emergency response to the property could involve ensuring unobstructed access to the main building by maintaining the front driveway in a condition that is clear of vehicles and debris. Introducing additional access points or gates in the perimeter fencing may improve responder access and movement, especially towards the rear of the property where access is currently limited. Strategically trimming or organizing the landscaped area could improve visibility and navigability for responders, reducing travel time and supporting more effective focal operations. Clearly marked pathways and designated staging areas could further facilitate streamlined navigation and efficient emergency operations.

wildfire.conditions: Scattered Trees; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Open Terrain; Scattered Shrubs; Vegetated Perimeter; Defensible Space; Tree Cluster; Sparse Vegetation
wildfire.recommendations: Enhancing defensible space by managing the vegetation adjacent to the structure could help reduce structure exposure to potential wildfire. Increasing the separation between scattered trees and the building can minimize the possibility of fire progression. Maintaining the open terrain while managing scattered shrubs may decrease fuel continuity and support suppression efforts. Managing the vegetated perimeter and maintaining sparse vegetation in this area can improve the long-term resilience of the property by reducing the potential for fire to encroach upon the structure. Additionally, managing the tree cluster to ensure adequate spacing and reducing excess vegetation nearby may further support firefighter access and mitigation operations.
